name: Type 2 Diabetes – High HbA1c Study
description: Adults with type 2 diabetes and elevated HbA1c.
onePageSummary:
OBJECTIVE: To evaluate the effectiveness of an intensified lifestyle and medication intervention in adults with type 2 diabetes and suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c ≥ 7.0%).

DESIGN: Randomized, controlled, 12-month trial.

ELIGIBILITY: Adults 18–75 years with documented type 2 diabetes and most recent HbA1c ≥ 7.0%. Exclusions: age > 80, end-stage renal disease, or other protocol-specified conditions.

INTERVENTION: Structured lifestyle counseling plus algorithm-driven medication titration vs. usual care.

OUTCOMES: Primary: change in HbA1c at 12 months. Secondary: weight, blood pressure, treatment satisfaction.

STATUS: Currently enrolling.

name: NEXUS
description: UC Davis ED study: validate decision instrument for blunt trauma A/P CT imaging.
onePageSummary:
Active UC Davis Emergency Department Research Study: NEXUS
Protocol: IRB# 2264128 | Expiration: 10/1/2025
Principal Investigator: Dr. David Dillon, MD
Study start date: October 2025

BACKGROUND: Unrecognized abdominal and pelvic injuries can produce permanent injury, severe disability and even death. Sporadic reports of "occult" injuries have generated concern, and physicians, fearing that they may fail to diagnose such injuries, have adopted the practice of obtaining computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis (A/P CT) on virtually all blunt trauma victims. This practice exposes large numbers of patients to radiation at considerable expense, while detecting injuries in a small minority of cases. Prior work suggests that a decision instrument, composed of a limited number of clinical criteria, can reliably identify blunt injury victims who have "no risk" of significant abdominal or pelvic injury (SAPI), and therefore no need for computed tomography (CT), without missing any injured patient. The investigators estimate that nation-wide implementation of this decision instrument could safely reduce CT imaging of blunt injury victims, reduce radiographic charges, and simultaneously decrease ionizing radiation exposure and the risk of radiation-induced malignancy.

PURPOSE: The investigators have already derived a decision instrument composed of select criteria that identifies injured patients with high sensitivity, while simultaneously identifying a subset of patients who can be safely spared imaging. The investigators' current goal is to validate this decision instrument. Validating the sensitivity of the decision instrument (validation phase) with high statistical confidence requires evaluations on approximately 6,600 blunt injury patients. Potential reductions in CT will be determined by counting the number of A/P CTs performed on "no risk" patients. Reductions in charges and radiation exposure will be determined by respectively summing radiographic charges and lifetime decreases in radiation morbidity and mortality for all "no risk" cases.

INCLUSION CRITERIA:
• Ages 18 years or older
• Any blunt injury victim who undergoes abdominopelvic imaging as part of their ED trauma evaluation

EXCLUSION CRITERIA: None

Consent required? No

Research Associate: Screen for eligible patients and notify Research Coordinator of potential subjects; assist Research Coordinator with getting data form filled out by ED Providers.

Research Coordinator contact: Logen Arndt, Abhi Gorhe, Nicholeth Santiago, Donovan Nielsen, Karina Klein, RN, Vocera "ED Research Coordinator"

name: HIP ATTACK-2
description: Expedited surgery for hip fracture patients with elevated troponin.
onePageSummary:
Active UC Davis Emergency Department Research Study: HIP ATTACK-2
Principal Investigators: Katren Tyler, M.D. (Emergency Medicine co-PI), Augustine Saiz, M.D. (Orthopedics co-PI)
Study start date: 7/15/2024 | Protocol: IRB# 1980210

BACKGROUND: Millions of adults worldwide suffer hip fractures annually, primarily affecting the elderly and often leading to severe complications (cardiovascular, venous thrombotic, infectious, bleeding), which can result in death. The 90-day mortality rate post-hip fracture is 10–20%. About 20–25% of patients show evidence of heart injury (elevated troponin) at admission; HIP ATTACK-1 suggested these patients may benefit from expedited surgery.

PURPOSE: HIP ATTACK-2 aims to provide more comprehensive evidence on the benefits and risks of expedited surgery for hip fracture patients, potentially influencing clinical guidelines. The trial evaluates mortality, major complications, and functional recovery.

INCLUSION CRITERIA:
• Age ≥ 45 years
• Patient is diagnosed with a hip fracture requiring surgery resulting from a low-energy mechanism (e.g., ground-level falls)
• The patient has an elevated troponin lab result at any point following their injury (abnormal = exceeds upper reference limit, e.g. > 19 ng/L)
• Capable of providing written informed consent

EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
• Currently taking an anticoagulant for which no reversing agent is available
• Currently taking a therapeutic vitamin K antagonist with a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
• Peri-prosthetic fracture, open fracture, or bilateral fractures
• Requiring emergency surgery for another reason (e.g., subdural hematoma)
• Acute MI with mechanical complication, STEMI, or cardiogenic shock
• Previously enrolled in HIP ATTACK-2

Note: Cannot enroll children, prisoners, or pregnant women. Screening 0700–1500 weekdays.

Research Coordinator: Donovan Nielsen. On-duty ED research coordinator: Vocera "ED Research Coordinator"

inclusionCriteria.description: Diagnosis of hip fracture (femoral neck, intertrochanteric, or subtrochanteric)
Elevated troponin after injury (above upper reference limit)

name: Takeda REVERSER (TAK-330)
description: Reversal of direct oral Factor Xa inhibitor–induced anticoagulation in patients requiring urgent surgery.
onePageSummary:
Active UC Davis Emergency Department Research Study: Takeda REVERSER
A Study of TAK-330 for Reversal of Direct Oral Factor Xa Inhibitor-induced Anticoagulation
Principal Investigator: Daniel Nishijima, MD, MAS
Study start: 9/25/2023 | Anticipated completion: 11/30/2025 | IRB #: 1934506

BACKGROUND: Patients on Factor Xa inhibitors are at high risk of bleeding, especially during urgent surgery. There is an unmet need for effective reversal agents.

PURPOSE: To compare TAK-330 with four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) for anticoagulation reversal in patients on Factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) requiring urgent surgery or invasive procedures.

INCLUSION: ≥18 years; currently on oral Factor Xa inhibitor; in surgeon's opinion subject requires urgent surgery/procedure with high-risk intraoperative bleeding within 15 hours of last Factor Xa dose; requires reversal agent for suspected Factor Xa inhibitor–related coagulopathy. If surgery beyond 15 hr: plasma anti-FXa >75 ng/mL at screening (apixaban/rivaroxaban only). Women of childbearing potential: negative pregnancy test prior to enrollment.

EXCLUSION: Expected survival <30 days; within 90 days: VTE, MI, DIC, ischemic stroke, TIA, hospitalization for unstable angina, severe/critical COVID-19; active major bleeding; polytrauma; prothrombotic/bleeding disorders; platelet <50,000/μL; HIT; procoagulant drugs/blood products within 7 days; heparin (UFH within 2 hr or LMWH within 6 hr); hypersensitivity to PCC or TAK-330; IgA deficiency with hypersensitivity; septic shock; liver failure Child-Pugh C; renal failure requiring dialysis; breastfeeding; other protocol exclusions.

Consent required: Yes. Research Coordinator: Donovan Nielsen (916-703-5645, dsnielsen@ucdavis.edu). On-duty ED research coordinator: Vocera "ED Research Coordinator".

inclusionCriteria.description: Currently on oral Factor Xa inhibitor (rivaroxaban, apixaban, or edoxaban)
